lesson:
The kanji 美 is 羊 (sheep) over 大 (big). A big, fat sheep — the ancient Chinese ideal of beauty was abundance, health, something well-fed and thriving. Beauty as vitality, not as thinness or fragility. That origin still echoes: 美味しい (おいしい, delicious — literally "beautiful-tasting") uses the same 美.

美しい is formal, literary beauty. Five syllables — u-tsu-ku-shi-i — that roll out with a weight and deliberateness that matches the word's register. You wouldn't casually say 美しい about your friend's new haircut. You'd use きれい instead. 美しい is for landscapes, music, prose, things that deserve to be admired with some distance and reverence: 美しい景色 (beautiful scenery), 美しい音楽 (beautiful music).

美しい vs きれい: Both mean "beautiful" but they occupy different spaces. きれい (a な-adjective) is everyday beauty — pretty, clean, neat. 美しい is elevated beauty — sublime, striking, worthy of art. きれい also means "clean" (a room can be きれい), which 美しい never does. When you want to describe something that stops you in your tracks, 美しい is the word.

Dictionaryうつくしい
Negativeうつくしくない
Pastうつくしかった
Past negうつくしくなかった
Adverbうつくしく
て-formうつくしくて

You've already met いつ (when) and its shapeshifting trick with particles. いつも is いつ + も — "when-also," meaning every time, always. The も particle adds inclusiveness: every possible "when" is included.

いつも describes habitual actions and constant states. いつも早く起きる (I always wake up early). いつもありがとう (thanks, as always). 彼はいつも遅い (he's always late). It sits naturally before the verb or adjective it modifies, and it needs no particle — it works as a standalone adverb.

Frequency scale: Japanese has a clean spectrum of frequency adverbs. いつも (always, 100%) → たいてい (usually, ~80%) → よく (often, ~60%) → 時々 (ときどき, sometimes, ~40%) → あまり (not very often, ~20%, + negative verb) → 全然 (ぜんぜん, not at all, 0%, + negative verb). Place いつも at the top and everything else falls into line below it.

The kanji 足 was originally a picture of a knee joint with a foot below it — the whole lower limb, not just the foot. That's exactly how あし works: it means both leg and foot, with no clean division between them. Where English draws a hard line at the ankle, Japanese sees one continuous structure. 足が長い can mean "long legs" (a compliment) or is about the feet depending on context.

As a radical and in compounds, 足 read as そく takes on an additional meaning: "sufficient, enough." 満足 (まんぞく, satisfaction — being full-footed, having enough), 不足 (ふそく, shortage — not enough). This isn't random — the idea of a foot stepping forward toward a goal connects to reaching sufficiency, having enough to stand on.

足 vs 脚: When you need to specify, 脚 (あし, same reading) focuses on the leg portion — particularly in contexts like furniture legs (テーブルの脚) or the legs of a race. But in everyday conversation, 足 handles everything. One kanji, both appendages, no complications.

起こす is the transitive half of a pair. Its partner 起きる (おきる) means to wake up, to get up — something you do yourself. 起こす means you make something rise. The most concrete use: 子供を起こす (wake the child up). You cause them to go from sleeping to awake.

But 起こす extends far beyond the alarm clock. 事故を起こす (cause an accident). 問題を起こす (cause a problem). 革命を起こす (start a revolution). The common thread: you raise something into existence, you set something in motion. Waking someone up is just the most literal version of bringing something from dormancy to action.

The kanji 起 is 走 (run) on the left and 己 (self) on the right. Running yourself up — rising to your feet, getting going. The energy of standing up and starting to move lives in this character.

Particles: 起こす takes を — the person or thing you wake/cause is your direct object. 子供を起こす. 事件を起こす.

Potential が起こせる: が marks what can be caused/woken. この音で子供が起こせる (this sound can wake the child).

Passive に起こされる: に marks who woke you. 朝6時に母に起こされた (I was woken up by my mother at 6 AM).

Causative に起こさせる: に marks who you make do the waking. 姉に弟を起こさせる (have the older sister wake up the younger brother).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 起きる (おきる)
Dictionaryおこすを

Politeおこしますを
Polite negおこしませんを

て-formおこしてを
Volitionalおこそうを

Causativeおこさせるに／を
Caus-passおこさせられるに
Potentialおこせるが
Imperativeおこせを
Imp なさいおこしなさいを
Cond ～ばおこせばを
Cond ～たらおこしたらを

見せる is the causative-turned-standalone-verb of 見る (to see). Historically, it literally meant "to make someone see" — and that's still exactly what it means. But it's been used so long and so often that it functions as its own independent verb, not as a grammatical causative form. (The formal causative of 見る would be 見させる, which sounds more forced.)

写真を見せる (show a photo). パスポートを見せてください (please show your passport). 力を見せる (show one's strength). The object of 見せる is the thing being shown, and the person you show it to takes に.

Pattern: 〜てみせる attached to another verb's て-form means "I'll show you I can do it" — a declaration of intent with a hint of defiance. 必ず合格してみせる (I'll pass, just you watch). やってみせる (I'll do it — and prove it). It's confident, almost theatrical. Compare with 〜てみる (try doing to see what happens) — very different nuance despite the similar sound.

Particles: 見せる takes を for the thing shown and に for the person you show it to. 友達に写真を見せる (show photos to a friend).

Potential が見せられる: が marks what can be shown. この書類が見せられない (I can't show this document).

Passive に見せられる: に marks who showed it. 怖い映画を見せられた (I was made to watch a scary movie).

Causative に見せさせる: making someone show something. Rare in practice — 見せる already contains causative meaning.

一段 Ichidan他動詞

Politeみせますを
Polite negみせませんを

て-formみせてを
Volitionalみせようを

Causativeみせさせるに／を
Caus-passみせさせられるに

Imperativeみせろを
Imp なさいみせなさいを
Cond ～ばみせればを
Cond ～たらみせたらを

The kanji 娘 is 女 (woman) plus 良 (good). A good woman — which in the original context meant a young, unmarried woman. The kanji wears its cultural history openly: "daughter" and "good woman" were the same concept in the society that created this character. Modern Japanese has largely moved past that equation, but the kanji remains.

むすめ carries two meanings that overlap but aren't identical. Meaning one: your daughter — うちの娘 (my daughter). Meaning two: a young woman or girl — あの娘 (that girl), often written あの子 in casual use. The "daughter" meaning is primary in most contexts, but don't be surprised to see 娘 used for any young woman in older or more literary writing.

Family pair: 娘 (daughter) and 息子 (むすこ, son) form a natural pair. Both are humble terms — you use them for your own children when speaking to others. For someone else's daughter, the respectful term is お嬢さん (おじょうさん). This humble/respectful split is typical of Japanese family vocabulary.

The kanji 楽 originally depicted a drum mounted on a wooden stand — an instrument of music and celebration. From music came pleasure, from pleasure came ease. That's why 楽 has such a wide range: 楽しい (fun), 楽 (easy/comfortable), 音楽 (music). They're all branches of the same tree — the joy that comes from a drumbeat.

楽しむ is the verb form — to actively enjoy something, to take pleasure in it. 旅行を楽しむ (enjoy the trip). 人生を楽しむ (enjoy life). 食事を楽しむ (enjoy a meal). Unlike the adjective 楽しい (which describes the state of being fun), the verb implies deliberate engagement — you're choosing to find joy in the experience.

Common expression: 楽しみにしている (I'm looking forward to it) uses the noun form 楽しみ (pleasure, anticipation). This is one of the most useful phrases in Japanese — memorize it as a unit. パーティーを楽しみにしている (I'm looking forward to the party).

Particles: 楽しむ takes を — the experience is your direct object. 休みを楽しむ (enjoy the holiday).

Potential が楽しめる: が marks what can be enjoyed. ここで景色が楽しめる (the scenery can be enjoyed here).

Passive に楽しまれる: に marks who enjoys it. 世界中の人に楽しまれている (enjoyed by people around the world).

Causative に楽しませる: に marks who you let enjoy. お客さんを楽しませる (entertain the guests). Note: 楽しませる is commonly used as "to entertain" — making someone experience enjoyment.

五段 Godan他動詞

Politeたのしみますを
Polite negたのしみませんを

て-formたのしんでを

Caus-passたのしませられるに

Imperativeたのしめを
Imp なさいたのしみなさいを
Cond ～ばたのしめばを
Cond ～たらたのしんだらを

The kanji 色 originally depicted a person kneeling over another person — the posture of intimacy. Its oldest meaning wasn't "color" at all, but "sexual desire, passion, beauty." The shift to "color" came through the idea of complexion — the flush of emotion on skin, the visual expression of feeling. That older, sensual meaning never fully left: 色っぽい (いろっぽい, sexy/alluring) and 色気 (いろけ, sex appeal) use 色 in its original sense.

As "color," いろ is the basic, all-purpose word. 何色？ (なにいろ, what color?). 赤い色 (red color). 色が変わる (the color changes). Japanese color words attach directly to 色: 赤色, 青色, 黄色 (きいろ, yellow — notably, the only basic color that requires 色 even in its short form).

色 also appears in 色々 (いろいろ, various/all sorts of) — literally "colors and colors," meaning many different kinds. This is one of the most common words in Japanese, and its connection to 色 reveals how Japanese sees variety: different colors, different types, all mixed together.

みんな comes from 皆 (みな), with the ん inserted for easier pronunciation. Both forms exist: みな is slightly more formal, みんな is casual and warm. In writing, you'll sometimes see the kanji 皆, but みんな in hiragana is far more common in everyday use.

The kanji 皆 is 比 (compare, lined up side by side) over 白 (white/clear). People standing in a row, all visible — everyone present, nobody excluded. That's the core meaning: the whole group, no exceptions.

みんな is grammatically flexible. It can be the subject: みんな来た (everyone came). It can modify with の: みんなの意見 (everyone's opinion). And it can function adverbially meaning "all": お菓子をみんな食べた (ate all the snacks). This last usage — "all" rather than "everyone" — trips people up. みんな can refer to all of something, not just all people.

Polite form: 皆さん (みなさん) adds the respectful さん suffix. This is what a teacher says to a class, what a speaker says to an audience, what an announcement uses to address the public. みんな is friends. 皆さん is everyone else.

The kanji 取 is 耳 (ear) plus 又 (hand). A hand grabbing an ear. This isn't metaphorical — in ancient warfare, soldiers would take the ears of defeated enemies as proof of their kills. The kanji preserves that brutal origin: to take, to seize, to claim.

Modern 取る has shed the violence but kept the breadth. It's one of the most overloaded verbs in Japanese: 写真を取る (take a photo — though 撮る is more specific). 休みを取る (take time off). メモを取る (take notes). 年を取る (to age — "take years"). 席を取る (save/take a seat). The verb doesn't care whether you're taking a physical object, an abstract concept, or a span of time.

取る vs 撮る vs 採る vs 盗る: All read とる. 取る is the default — general taking. 撮る is specifically for photos and video. 採る is harvesting, collecting, or adopting (measures). 盗る is stealing (though 盗む is more common for that). In casual writing, 取る or hiragana とる covers everything.

Particles: 取る takes を — the thing taken is your direct object. 塩を取って (pass the salt — take it for me).

Potential が取れる: が marks what can be taken. 休みが取れる (I can take time off). Note: 取れる also means "to come off" as an intransitive — ボタンが取れた (the button came off).

Passive に取られる: に marks who took it. 猫に魚を取られた (the cat took my fish).

Causative に…を取らせる: に marks who you make take something. 生徒にメモを取らせる (have students take notes).

五段 Godan他動詞

勉 means "to exert oneself" and 強 means "strong, to force." Together: forced exertion. Studying as an act of willpower, of pushing yourself. The word doesn't pretend studying is fun — it's baked into the kanji that this is work. And in a culture where students regularly study until midnight for entrance exams, the honesty is appropriate.

In Chinese, 勉強 (miǎnqiǎng) means "reluctant" or "forced" — the original meaning of pushing yourself to do something you'd rather not. Japanese took that core sense and narrowed it to the most common thing people force themselves to do: study. The character 強 shows up in 強い (つよい, strong) and 無理強い (むりじい, coercion) — the "force" meaning runs through all its uses.

勉強する is a する-noun — add する and it becomes a verb: 日本語を勉強する (study Japanese). You'll also encounter 勉強になる ("it becomes studying" → it's educational, I learned something from it) — a polite way of saying something was informative. Use it after someone teaches you something and you'll sound appreciative.

meaning: to be able to, be completed
lesson:
できる is two verbs fused into one word. Meaning one: ability — "can do." 日本語ができる (I can speak Japanese). 料理ができる (I can cook). Meaning two: completion — "to be done, to come into existence." 準備ができた (preparations are done). 友達ができた (I made a friend — a friend came into being). Both meanings connect: if something can be done, it gets done. Ability and completion are two sides of one coin.

できる is technically the potential form of する (to do) — but it has become so independent that most speakers don't even think of it that way. It conjugates as its own verb: できない (can't), できた (could / was completed), できます (polite).

Key particle: できる takes が, not を. The ability is framed as a property of the thing, not as something you do: 英語ができる (English is possible for me). This is the same が-subject pattern as 分かる and 好き. The thing that can be done is the grammatical subject.

できるだけ: できるだけ早く (as fast as possible). This phrase — "to the extent that can be done" — is extremely common and worth memorizing as a block.

Causativeできさせるを／に

The kanji 短 is 矢 (arrow) plus 豆 (bean). An arrow as small as a bean — short. The arrow component connects to measurement (arrows were used as units of length in ancient China), and the bean signals smallness. The combination gives you "short in length."

短い covers length, duration, and distance. 短いスカート (short skirt). 短い時間 (short time). 短い距離 (short distance). It pairs with 長い (ながい, long) as natural opposites. But notice: 短い is about measurable extent, not about height. A short person isn't 短い — they're 背が低い (せがひくい, low in stature). This is a common mistake for English speakers.

Sound: mi-ji-ka-i — four syllables for a word meaning "short." Japanese loves this kind of irony. The word for long, 長い (ながい), is actually shorter in syllable count. Don't look for logic in word length matching meaning — but it does make a decent mnemonic: みじかい is so long it overflows its own meaning.

Dictionaryみじかい
Negativeみじかくない
Pastみじかかった
Past negみじかくなかった
Adverbみじかく
て-formみじかくて

The kanji 落 is the grass radical (艹) on top, 氵 (water) on the left, and 各 (each/every) below. The image: water drops falling from leaves — rain dripping off foliage. From that concrete picture came the abstract meaning of falling, dropping, declining.

落ちる is intransitive — things fall by themselves. Its transitive partner is 落とす (おとす, to drop something). りんごが落ちる (an apple falls) vs りんごを落とす (drop an apple). The distinction matters everywhere: 成績が落ちる (grades fall — they decline on their own) vs 成績を落とす (let grades drop — you caused it).

Critical meaning: 落ちる means "to fail" an exam. 試験に落ちた (I failed the exam). This is the standard word — not 失敗する, which is for general failure. Exam failure is falling. And its opposite? 受かる (うかる, to pass) — to float up, to be accepted. You either fall or rise.

Particles: 落ちる takes が for the thing that falls. 葉が落ちる (leaves fall). For failing an exam: 試験に落ちる — the に marks what you fell at.

Passive に落ちられる: rare in the literal sense. More commonly heard in derived expressions.

Causative を／に落ちさせる: making something fall. In practice, 落とす (the transitive partner) is used instead.

息 (breath, life) + 子 (child) = son. Literally, your "breath-child" — the child who carries your life force forward. The kanji 息 is 自 (nose/self) over 心 (heart): breath flowing from nose to heart, the vital connection between body and spirit. Calling your son your 息子 frames him as the continuation of your very breath.

Like 娘 (daughter), 息子 is humble — you use it for your own son when speaking to others. For someone else's son, the respectful term is 息子さん (むすこさん) with the polite suffix, or more formally ご子息 (ごしそく).

An interesting asymmetry: 娘 can mean both "daughter" and "young woman," but 息子 only means "son." There's no parallel use of 息子 to refer to young men in general. For that, Japanese uses 男の子 (おとこのこ, boy) or 若者 (わかもの, young person). The word is specific to the parent-child relationship.

The kanji 白 is believed to depict the sun just above the horizon — the first pale light of dawn, before color fills the sky. White as the absence of color, the blank canvas before the world paints itself. In oracle bone script, it was a small circle (the sun) sitting on a horizontal line (the horizon). Dawn light.

白い covers both the color white and the concept of blankness or emptiness. 白い紙 (white paper / blank paper). 白い壁 (white wall). And in the extended sense: 白黒 (しろくろ, black and white — right and wrong). 白状する (はくじょうする, to confess — to make things white/clear). White in Japanese culture is associated with purity and honesty, but also with death — funeral colors in Japan are white, not black.

白 as a noun: 白 (しろ) without the い is the noun form — the color white itself. 白が好き (I like white). This noun/adjective pair exists for several colors: 白/白い, 黒/黒い (くろ/くろい, black), 赤/赤い (あか/あかい, red), 青/青い (あお/あおい, blue). These four are the oldest color words in Japanese — the only colors that have native い-adjective forms.

Dictionaryしろい
Negativeしろくない
Pastしろかった
Past negしろくなかった
Adverbしろく
て-formしろくて

Three kanji, each pulling its weight: 飛 (fly) + 行 (go/travel) + 機 (machine). A flying-traveling-machine. Japanese compound nouns can be remarkably literal, and this is a perfect example — no metaphor, no ambiguity, just a machine that goes by flying.

You already know 飛ぶ (to fly) and 行く (to go). The third kanji, 機, is one of the most productive in the language. It means "machine" or "mechanism" and shows up everywhere: 洗濯機 (せんたくき, washing machine), 携帯電話機 (mobile phone device), 機会 (きかい, opportunity — a mechanism of timing). When you see 機 at the end of a word, expect a device or apparatus.

The Japanese relationship with 飛行機 is deeply practical — Japan is an island nation, and for international travel there's no alternative. 飛行機に乗る (board a plane — literally "ride" a plane) is the standard expression. 飛行機が飛ぶ (the plane flies) uses 飛ぶ from earlier in this list — full circle.

病 (sick) + 気 (energy/spirit) = illness. But the combination tells a deeper story than "sick" + "energy." In traditional East Asian medicine, illness was understood as a disorder of 気 — the vital energy flowing through the body. When your 気 is blocked, stagnant, or depleted, you're sick. 病気 isn't just "disease" — it's "sick energy," your life force gone wrong.

The kanji 病 has the sickness radical 疒 — a person lying on a bed, seen from the side. Every illness-related kanji uses this radical: 痛 (いた, pain), 症 (しょう, symptom), 療 (りょう, treatment). Once you recognize 疒, you'll spot medical vocabulary instantly.

病気 is the general word for being sick. 病気になる (get sick — "become illness"). 病気が治る (recover from illness — "the sickness heals"). 病気で休む (take time off due to illness). Note the particle: で marks the reason. You're absent because of illness, not at illness.

The kanji 冬 in its oldest form showed two lines resembling knotted threads hanging down — the end of something, the final tying-off. Winter is the end of the year's cycle, the season of closure. The two dots at the bottom of the modern form (冫) are the ice radical, added later to reinforce the cold. Ice under a symbol of endings — winter.

Japan has four sharply defined seasons, and they structure the culture in ways that go far beyond weather. 冬 is the season of 鍋 (なべ, hot pot), 温泉 (おんせん, hot springs), こたつ (heated table), and New Year celebrations. Seasonal vocabulary matters in Japanese conversation — people expect you to talk about the seasons and how they affect daily life.

The four seasons: 春 (はる, spring), 夏 (なつ, summer), 秋 (あき, autumn), 冬 (ふゆ, winter). All four are single-kanji words with native Japanese readings — these are ancient, fundamental vocabulary. Learn them as a set. 冬休み (ふゆやすみ, winter vacation) is one of the most common compounds — the break around New Year.

The kanji 年 was originally a picture of a person carrying a bundle of grain — the harvest. One harvest cycle = one year. Time measured not by calendars but by crops. The connection between 年 and agriculture is ancient, and it survives in 豊年 (ほうねん, abundant harvest year).

とし means both "year" and "age" — and the two meanings blend seamlessly. 年を取る (to age — "take years"). 年が明ける (the new year arrives — "the year opens"). いい年して (at your age! — mild scolding for age-inappropriate behavior). Time passing and growing older are the same word because, in Japanese thinking, they're the same thing.

Readings: とし is the native reading for standalone use. ねん is the Chinese-derived reading used in compounds and as a counter: 三年 (さんねん, three years), 去年 (きょねん, last year), 来年 (らいねん, next year), 今年 (ことし, this year — note the irregular reading, not こんねん). The ことし reading is one of those old native words that refused to be replaced by the Chinese compound.

tags: 重い vs 軽い pair
lesson:
The kanji 重 is dense in every sense. It started as a picture of a person standing on the ground with a heavy bundle on their back — the vertical line is the person, the horizontal strokes are the weight pressing down. Later it got reinterpreted as layers stacking up, which is why 重 also means "to layer" or "to pile" in compounds: 重ねる (かさねる, to stack/layer), 二重 (にじゅう, double/twofold). Heaviness and repetition — things that pile up get heavy.

おもい goes well beyond kilograms. A heavy atmosphere: 空気が重い. A heavy mood: 気分が重い. A heavy responsibility: 責任が重い. A serious illness: 病気が重い. In every case, the word carries a sense of something pressing down on you, physically or psychologically. When someone says あの人は重い about a romantic partner, it means they're emotionally clingy — too much weight on the relationship.

Sound link: おもい shares its first two sounds with 思い (おもい, feelings/thoughts). They're different words with different kanji, but the overlap isn't entirely accidental — in classical Japanese, deep feelings were described as heavy things that weigh on the heart. The pun between "heavy" and "deep feelings" runs through Japanese poetry.

Dictionaryおもい
Negativeおもくない
Pastおもかった
Past negおもくなかった
Adverbおもく
て-formおもくて

The kanji 胸 has the meat/flesh radical 月 (originally 肉) on the left — the same radical in 腕 (arm), 肩 (shoulder), 腰 (waist). Any kanji with this radical is almost certainly a body part or has something to do with flesh. The right side 匈 was borrowed for its sound in ancient Chinese.

But むね isn't just anatomy. It's where emotions live. 胸が痛い isn't a cardiology problem — it means your heart aches, you're emotionally hurt. 胸がいっぱい (chest is full) means you're overwhelmed with emotion, too moved to speak. 胸がドキドキする (chest goes thump-thump) is the physical sensation of excitement or nervousness. Where English puts feelings in the "heart," Japanese puts them in the 胸 — the whole chest cavity, not just the organ.

胸を張る (to puff out one's chest) means to stand tall with pride. 胸に手を当てて考える (put your hand on your chest and think) means to search your conscience. The physical gesture maps directly to the emotional meaning.

払う originally had nothing to do with money. The oldest meaning was "to brush away, to sweep off" — clearing away something unwanted. You still see this in ほこりを払う (brush off dust) and 手で払う (swat away with your hand). The jump to "pay" makes sense when you think of it as clearing a debt — sweeping away what you owe. The kanji 払 has the hand radical 扌 and ム plus ル — a hand pushing something away.

This dual meaning is still alive. お金を払う (pay money) and 注意を払う (pay attention — literally "sweep out your awareness") and 犠牲を払う (pay a sacrifice) all use the same verb. The connecting thread: you're giving something up, sending it away from yourself.

Neighborhood: For buying things, you'll hear both 払う and 出す (to put out) around money. 払う emphasizes settling the debt. お金を出す emphasizes physically producing the cash. At a restaurant, ここは私が払う means "I'll cover this" — the debt, the obligation. It's about who clears the bill, not who pulls out the wallet.

Particles: 払う takes を — the money or attention or effort being expended is the direct object. お金を払う. 注意を払う. You can add に for the recipient: 店に払う (pay to the shop).

Potential が払える: が — what you can pay becomes the subject. この金額が払える？ (can you pay this amount?).

Passive に払われる: に marks the payer. 給料が会社に払われる (salary is paid by the company).

Causative に…を払わせる: に marks who you make pay, を marks what. 後輩に払わせる (make the junior pay).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryはらうを
Negativeはらわないを
Politeはらいますを
Polite negはらいませんを
Pastはらったを
Polite pastはらいましたを
て-formはらってを
Volitionalはらおうを
Polite volはらいましょうを
Passiveはらわれるに
Causativeはらわせるに／を
Caus-passはらわせられるに
Potentialはらえるが
Imperativeはらえを
Imp なさいはらいなさいを
Cond ～ばはらえばを
Cond ～たらはらったらを

tags: 重い vs 軽い pair
lesson:
軽い is the exact opposite of 重い, and learning them as a pair is the right move. Heavy and light, 重い and 軽い — they show up together constantly. The kanji 軽 has the vehicle radical 車 on the left. A light vehicle, easy to pull. The right side originally suggested something small and nimble. Cars in Japan still use this kanji: 軽自動車 (けいじどうしゃ, a "kei car" — those tiny boxy cars everywhere in Japan, taxed at a lower rate because they're small and light).

Like 重い, 軽い stretches far beyond physical weight. 軽い食事 (a light meal). 軽い気持ち (a casual attitude — not taking things seriously). 口が軽い (loose-lipped — literally "light mouth," someone who can't keep secrets). 軽い冗談 (a light joke). The metaphor works the same way as English: lightness means ease, casualness, and sometimes a lack of substance or seriousness.

Sound anchor: ka-ru-i bounces — three light syllables that feel effortless to say. Compare with o-mo-i, which sits heavier in the mouth. The sounds match the meanings.

Dictionaryかるい
Negativeかるくない
Pastかるかった
Past negかるくなかった
Adverbかるく
て-formかるくて

You already know 見る — to see. 見つける adds つける (to attach, to fix onto) to create something more active: not just seeing, but locking on to something. Finding is seeing that sticks. Your eyes land on what you were looking for and fix there.

The intransitive partner 見つかる (みつかる) is just as common, and the distinction matters. 見つける: you find something — you're the active searcher. 見つかる: something gets found — it turns up on its own, or your search succeeds from the thing's perspective. 鍵を見つけた (I found the key — I did the finding). 鍵が見つかった (the key was found — it turned up). Japanese often prefers 見つかる because it avoids claiming personal credit — the thing just happened to appear.

Neighborhood: 見つける vs 探す (さがす, to search for). Searching is the process; finding is the result. 探して見つけた (searched and found) — a natural pair. You can also discover things you weren't searching for: 偶然見つけた (found it by accident). That's when 見つける feels closest to English "discover."

Particles: 見つける takes を — the thing you find is your direct object. 仕事を見つける (find a job). 答えを見つける (find the answer).

Potential が見つけられる: が — what can be found becomes the subject. In practice, people often just use 見つかる instead of the potential form.

Passive に見つけられる: に marks the finder. 先生に見つけられた (I was found by the teacher — caught).

Causative に…を見つけさせる: に marks who you make find, を marks the thing. 子供に自分で答えを見つけさせる (make the kid find the answer themselves).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 見つかる (みつかる)

Negativeみつけないを
Politeみつけますを
Polite negみつけませんを
Pastみつけたを

て-formみつけてを
Volitionalみつけようを
Polite volみつけましょうを
Passiveみつけられるに
Causativeみつけさせるに／を
Caus-passみつけさせられるに

Imperativeみつけろを
Imp なさいみつけなさいを
Cond ～ばみつければを
Cond ～たらみつけたらを

The kanji 忘 is 亡 (to perish, to be gone) over 心 (heart/mind). Something dies in your mind — that's forgetting. It's a beautifully literal construction. The same 亡 appears in 死亡 (しぼう, death) and 亡くなる (なくなる, to pass away). When you forget, the memory perishes.

忘れる covers two English concepts: forgetting information and physically leaving something behind. 名前を忘れた (forgot the name). 傘を忘れた (left my umbrella behind). In both cases, something that should be with you — a memory, an object — isn't. Japanese doesn't feel the need to distinguish between mental and physical forgetting.

Key pattern: 忘れないで and 忘れずに — both mean "don't forget" but hit differently. 忘れないで is a direct request (casual). 忘れずに is more formal and written — you'll see it on signs and in instructions. The construction 〜ずに is a classical negative that survived into modern Japanese for exactly these kinds of polite directives.

Pair: The opposite of 忘れる is 覚える (おぼえる, to memorize/remember) — word #130 in your queue. They're natural partners: 覚えたのに忘れた (I memorized it but forgot).

Particles: 忘れる takes を — the thing you forget is the direct object. 約束を忘れる (forget a promise). 電車に傘を忘れる (leave an umbrella on the train — に marks the location).

Potential が忘れられる: が — the thing that can be forgotten becomes the subject. あの日のことが忘れられない (I can't forget that day) — one of the most common ways this verb gets used.

Passive に忘れられる: に marks who forgets you. みんなに忘れられた (I was forgotten by everyone).

Causative に…を忘れさせる: に marks who you make forget, を marks what. 嫌なことを忘れさせてくれる (it helps me forget unpleasant things).

Imperativeわすれろを

Cond ～ばわすれればを
Cond ～たらわすれたらを

The kanji 酒 is 氵 (water) plus 酉 — and 酉 is a picture of a wine jar. A jar of liquid: alcohol. The 酉 radical shows up in almost every alcohol-related kanji: 醸 (to brew), 酔 (drunk), 酢 (vinegar — fermentation gone too far). Once you recognize this jar, you'll spot it everywhere.

In everyday Japanese, お酒 (with the honorific お) is the default word for any alcoholic drink — beer, wine, whiskey, everything. Without context, it doesn't specifically mean sake/rice wine. If someone asks お酒を飲みますか, they're asking if you drink alcohol in general, not offering you a cup of nihonshu. When you want to be specific about Japanese rice wine, say 日本酒 (にほんしゅ).

The word carries social weight. お酒の席 (a drinking occasion) is a recognized social institution in Japan — after-work drinking, 飲み会 (のみかい), is where real opinions come out and bonds form. お酒に弱い (weak with alcohol — a lightweight) and お酒に強い (strong with alcohol — can hold their liquor) are among the first things people learn about each other socially.

どちら is the polite form of どっち — both mean "which one" when choosing between two options. どちらがいいですか (which one would you prefer?). The ど prefix marks it as a question word in the ko-so-a-do system: こちら (this way/this one), そちら (that way/that one), あちら (that way over there), どちら (which way/which one).

But どちら has a second life as a polite "where." どちらからいらっしゃいましたか (where did you come from?) is formal speech you'd hear at a reception desk. And どちら様ですか (who are you? — literally "which person") is the most polite way to ask someone's identity, far softer than だれですか. The indirectness is the politeness — instead of bluntly asking "who," you gently ask "which direction," as if the person is one of several possibilities.

The full set: どっち (casual, between friends), どちら (polite, safe for any situation), いずれ (formal/written, literary). For daily conversation, どちら is the one to reach for unless you're already on casual terms.

Japanese family terms come in pairs: a humble word for your own family member, and a polite word for someone else's. 姉 (あね) is the humble one — my older sister, used when talking about her to outsiders. The polite counterpart is お姉さん (おねえさん) — used for someone else's older sister, or to address your own sister directly. You'd never introduce your sister at a formal event as お姉さん — that would be elevating your own family, which violates the うち／そと principle.

The kanji 姉 has 女 (woman) on the left and 市 on the right. The 女 radical runs through all the family-woman kanji: 妹 (younger sister), 母 (mother), 嫁 (bride). The right side was borrowed for sound, not meaning.

The full sibling map: 姉 (あね, my older sister) / お姉さん (someone's older sister). 兄 (あに, my older brother) / お兄さん. 妹 (いもうと, my younger sister) / 妹さん. 弟 (おとうと, my younger brother) / 弟さん. Japanese forces you to specify older or younger — there's no neutral "sister" or "brother." Birth order is baked into the vocabulary.

The kanji 覚 has 見 (to see) at the bottom — the eye-on-legs you already know. The top portion relates to consciousness and awareness. To 覚える is to bring something into your awareness, to make it stay where your mind can see it. The same kanji read differently gives you 覚ます (さます, to wake up — become aware) and 覚悟 (かくご, resolve/preparedness — your mind is fully awake to what's coming).

覚える has two distinct uses that beginners often mix up. First: to memorize — to deliberately commit something to memory. 単語を覚える (memorize vocabulary). 漢字を覚える (learn kanji by heart). Second: to remember — to hold a memory of something that happened. あの日のことを覚えている (I remember that day). The ている form is key here: 覚えている means "I have it memorized / I remember" — the ongoing state of having stored something.

覚える vs 思い出す: Both can translate to "remember," but 覚えている means the memory never left — it's been in your head the whole time. 思い出す (おもいだす) means to recall — to pull a memory back from somewhere it had receded to. まだ覚えている (I still remember) vs やっと思い出した (I finally recalled it).

Particles: 覚える takes を — the thing you memorize or remember is the direct object. 名前を覚える. 道を覚える (learn the route).

Potential が覚えられる: が — what you can memorize becomes the subject. こんなに多い漢字が覚えられない (I can't memorize this many kanji).

Passive に覚えられる: に marks who remembers you. 先生に名前を覚えられた (the teacher remembered my name).

Causative に…を覚えさせる: に marks who you make memorize, を marks what. 子供に九九を覚えさせる (make the kid memorize the times tables).

Cond ～ばおぼえればを
Cond ～たらおぼえたらを

The kanji 狭 has the dog radical 犭 on the left — which seems random until you learn that this radical originally depicted a crouching or constrained animal. Something pressed in, squeezed tight. The right side 夾 literally means "sandwiched between" — a person (大) pressed between two others. The whole character screams constriction.

せまい describes spaces that feel too small for their contents. この部屋は狭い (this room is cramped). 道が狭い (the road is narrow). It's subjective — a Tokyo apartment might be 狭い by American standards but perfectly normal by Tokyo standards. The word carries a mild complaint: this space should be bigger than it is.

狭い vs 小さい: 小さい means "small" in general — the object itself is small. 狭い means the space is small relative to what needs to fit in it. A 小さい部屋 is a small room (neutral observation). A 狭い部屋 is a cramped room (it feels tight). The opposite of 狭い is 広い (ひろい, spacious/wide). The opposite of 小さい is 大きい. Different axes entirely.

Beyond physical space: 心が狭い (narrow-hearted — petty, small-minded). 視野が狭い (narrow field of vision — narrow-minded). Cramped thinking, cramped generosity.

Dictionaryせまい
Negativeせまくない
Pastせまかった
Past negせまくなかった
Adverbせまく
て-formせまくて

The kanji 赤 combines 大 (big/person) and 火 (fire). A person on fire. That's as red as it gets. In oracle bone script, it was even more explicit — a figure standing over flames, face flushed from the heat. Red is the color of fire, blood, and embarrassment in every culture, and the kanji remembers that.

赤い is one of only a handful of basic color words that are true い-adjectives in Japanese. The full set: 赤い (red), 青い (blue), 白い (white), 黒い (black), 黄色い (yellow). These five conjugate like normal い-adjectives. Other colors — green, purple, orange — are nouns and need の to modify things (緑の葉, not 緑い葉). This distinction tells you something about which colors are old enough and fundamental enough to have earned their own adjective forms in the language.

赤 carries cultural weight beyond the color. 赤ちゃん (あかちゃん, baby — a "red one," because newborns are red-faced). 赤字 (あかじ, financial deficit — "red figures," same as English "in the red"). 赤の他人 (あかのたにん, a complete stranger — "red outsider," where 赤 intensifies to mean "utter, absolute"). That last usage is surprising — 赤 as an intensifier meaning "totally, completely" has no color in it at all.

Dictionaryあかい
Negativeあかくない
Pastあかかった
Past negあかくなかった
Adverbあかく
て-formあかくて

Japanese has no single word for "to wear." Instead, different verbs handle different body zones and accessory types. 着る covers the torso — shirts, jackets, dresses, anything you pull over your upper body. Below the waist: はく (pants, skirts, shoes, socks). On the head: かぶる (hats, helmets). Accessories: する or つける (watches, ties, earrings). This isn't an arbitrary hassle — it reflects how Japanese thinks about getting dressed as separate physical actions rather than one abstract concept.

The kanji 着 has 羊 (sheep) on top and 目 (eye) at the bottom, though these are historical distortions. The original meaning was "to arrive" — and that meaning is still the primary one in compounds: 到着 (とうちゃく, arrival). The connection to clothing: a garment "arrives" on your body, settles onto it. You'll see 着く (つく, to arrive at a place) far more often than 着る in written Japanese.

Critical: 着る is an ichidan verb despite ending in -iru and being only two mora. Don't confuse it with 切る (きる, to cut), which is godan. Same sound, completely different conjugation. 着ない (ichidan) vs 切らない (godan).

Particles: 着る takes を — the garment is the direct object. シャツを着る. コートを着る. For the ongoing state of wearing, use 着ている: 赤いシャツを着ている (is wearing a red shirt).

Potential が着られる: が — what can be worn becomes the subject. このドレスがまだ着られる (this dress can still be worn).

Passive に着られる: uncommon, but 服に着られている (being worn by the clothes — the clothes are wearing you) is a set phrase meaning the outfit overpowers the person, like a child in an oversized suit.

Causative に…を着させる: に marks who you dress, を marks the garment. 子供にコートを着させる (make the kid put on a coat).

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryきるを
Negativeきないを
Politeきますを
Polite negきませんを
Pastきたを
Polite pastきましたを
て-formきてを
Volitionalきようを
Polite volきましょうを
Passiveきられるに
Causativeきさせるに／を
Caus-passきさせられるに
Potentialきられるが
Imperativeきろを
Imp なさいきなさいを
Cond ～ばきればを
Cond ～たらきたらを

The kanji 笑 has the bamboo radical 竹 on top and 夭 (a person with arms up, bending) below. The bamboo connection is debated — one theory says bamboo bends in the wind like a person doubled over laughing. Another says the original character showed a person with their mouth wide open and the top was teeth, not bamboo. Either way, the image works: someone bending, mouth open, overcome.

笑う covers both laughing and smiling — Japanese doesn't force you to choose. 大声で笑う (laugh loudly). にっこり笑う (smile gently). Context and adverbs do the work of distinguishing. If you need to be specific, 微笑む (ほほえむ) is "to smile" exclusively — but it's literary. In conversation, 笑う handles everything.

Internet Japanese turned 笑 into an abbreviation: w or www (from わらう → w) is the Japanese equivalent of "lol." You'll see it everywhere in online comments: マジでwww. More w's = funnier. A field of w's looks like grass, which spawned the term 草 (くさ, grass) as slang for "that's hilarious" — 草生える (grass grows — I'm dying laughing).

Particles: 笑う is primarily intransitive — you just laugh, no direct object needed. みんなが笑った. But it can take を when you're laughing at someone: 人を笑う (laugh at someone — mock them). This を carries a sting — you're making someone the object of your laughter.

Passive に笑われる: に marks who laughs at you. みんなに笑われた (everyone laughed at me). This is the classic adversative passive — being laughed at is rarely pleasant.

Causative を／に笑わせる: を when you're making someone laugh (you're the comedian): お客を笑わせる (make the audience laugh). に is less common here but leans permissive — letting someone laugh.

て-formわらってを

Passiveわらわれるに
Causativeわらわせるを／に
Caus-passわらわせられるに
Potentialわらえるを
Imperativeわらえを
Imp なさいわらいなさいを
Cond ～ばわらえばを
Cond ～たらわらったらを

一番 is Japanese's cheat code for superlatives. English builds them with suffixes (-est) or "most" — Japanese just slaps 一番 in front of any adjective. 一番大きい (biggest). 一番好き (favorite — literally "#1 liked"). 一番いい (best). There's no irregular form, no memorization — just prefix 一番 and you're done. This is one of those places where Japanese is genuinely simpler than English.

The kanji break down as 一 (one) and 番 (number/turn/order). Literally "number one." 番 itself is a counter for ordinal positions: 二番 (second), 三番 (third). It also means "turn" as in taking turns: 次は私の番だ (next is my turn). The word 番号 (ばんごう, number — as in phone number or seat number) uses this same 番.

As a noun vs adverb: 一番 works both ways. As a noun: 一番になりたい (I want to be number one). As an adverb: 一番高い山 (the tallest mountain). No particle needed when it's modifying an adjective — it just goes directly before it.

授 means "to grant, to bestow" and 業 means "work, task, deed." Together: the bestowing of work — a class is where knowledge gets handed down from teacher to student. It's a top-down word. The teacher 授ける (bestows), and the student receives. This framing is baked into the word itself.

授業 specifically means class as an event — the scheduled block of time when teaching happens. 授業が始まる (class starts). 授業中 (during class). 授業をサボる (to skip class — サボる from French "sabotage"). It doesn't mean "class" in the sense of a group of students (that's クラス) or a course over a semester (that's 講義 (こうぎ) at the university level).

The 業 kanji is worth noting — it appears in 仕事-adjacent words constantly: 職業 (しょくぎょう, occupation), 作業 (さぎょう, work/task), 卒業 (そつぎょう, graduation — completing your work). It carries a sense of serious, sustained effort — not a hobby, not play.

The kanji 週 is the movement radical 辶 (road/advance) wrapping around 周 (circumference, cycle). A cycle that moves forward — one revolution of days, then the next begins. It's a clean visual: time going around and around.

On its own, 週 is rarely used as a standalone word. It lives in compounds: 今週 (こんしゅう, this week), 来週 (らいしゅう, next week), 先週 (せんしゅう, last week), 毎週 (まいしゅう, every week). The pattern is simple: slap a time-indicator prefix onto 週. The same prefixes work across other time words — 今月 (this month), 来年 (next year), 先月 (last month) — so learning them once unlocks the whole set.

As a counter: 一週間 (いっしゅうかん, one week). Note the 間 — you need it when counting duration. 二週間 (two weeks), 三週間 (three weeks). Without 間, 一週 means "one lap/cycle," which is why 一周 (using the base kanji 周 instead) means "one lap around."

You're learning 漢字 to learn 漢字. The word is self-referential in the best way. 漢 refers to the Han dynasty — the period of Chinese history so culturally dominant that "Han" became synonymous with "Chinese" in East Asian languages. 字 means "character, letter." Han characters. Characters from China.

The irony: Japanese couldn't exist without kanji, but kanji aren't Japanese. They were imported from China starting around the 5th century, and Japanese speakers had to perform an incredible hack — taking a writing system designed for a completely unrelated language and retrofitting it onto Japanese. That's why each kanji has both 音読み (おんよみ, the Chinese-derived reading) and 訓読み (くんよみ, the native Japanese reading). The Chinese sound came with the character; the Japanese meaning got bolted on later.

漢字 in modern Japan occupies a strange cultural space. There's a national 漢字検定 (かんじけんてい, Kanji Aptitude Test) that millions of people take voluntarily. Every December, Kiyomizu Temple in Kyoto announces the 今年の漢字 (kanji of the year) — a single character chosen to capture the national mood. Kanji aren't just a writing tool. They're a cultural institution.

Three kanji, each doing specific work: 自 (self) + 転 (revolve, turn) + 車 (vehicle). A self-revolving vehicle. You are the engine — you turn the wheels yourself. Compare with 自動車 (じどうしゃ, automobile): 自 (self) + 動 (move) + 車 (vehicle) — a self-moving vehicle, one that moves on its own. The difference between 転 (revolving by your effort) and 動 (moving on its own) is the whole distinction between a bicycle and a car, captured in a single kanji swap.

Japan is a bicycle country. There are roughly 70 million bicycles — nearly one for every two people. 自転車通勤 (じてんしゃつうきん, bicycle commuting) is common and unremarkable. ママチャリ (mama-chari) — the utilitarian city bike with a basket and child seat — is an icon of Japanese daily life. チャリ is the casual slang for bicycle, shortened from チャリンコ, which may come from the sound of a bicycle bell.

In casual speech, almost nobody says the full じてんしゃ when there's a shorter option. チャリ among friends, 自転車 in any remotely formal context.

電 (electricity) + 車 (vehicle) = electric vehicle. Simple enough — but 電車 specifically means an electric train, the urban and suburban kind that runs on tracks with overhead power lines. It doesn't cover every train. 新幹線 (しんかんせん, bullet train) is technically not a 電車 in strict usage — it's a 列車 (れっしゃ, a "lined-up vehicle," i.e., a train with multiple cars). And rural diesel trains are 汽車 (きしゃ). But in everyday Tokyo conversation, 電車 covers most train situations.

The 電 kanji appears in a constellation of modern-life words: 電話 (でんわ, telephone — electric talk), 電気 (でんき, electricity/light), 電子 (でんし, electronic). Anything powered by electricity gets this kanji. It originally depicted lightning — 雨 (rain) with a bolt flashing through it — and was repurposed for the modern concept of electricity.

Train culture is central to Japanese life. 電車に乗る (ride a train), 電車を降りる (get off a train), 電車に乗り遅れる (miss the train). If you live in Japan, these phrases become part of your daily operating system.

The kanji 探 has the hand radical 扌 and a right side that originally depicted reaching deep into a hole or cavity. Searching as a physical act — your hand groping in the dark for something you know is there but can't see. That tactile sense of fumbling and reaching is baked into the character.

探す is the process, not the result. You 探す (search), and if you're lucky, you 見つける (find). 仕事を探している (I'm looking for a job — still searching). 犯人を探す (search for the culprit). 言葉を探す (search for the right words — groping for what to say).

探す vs 捜す: Same pronunciation, different kanji, different nuance. 探す is general searching — looking for your keys, browsing for an apartment, trying to find a restaurant. 捜す is more serious — a police search, looking for a missing person, hunting for something that's been lost and needs to be recovered. In practice, 探す covers both in casual writing, and the distinction mostly matters in formal or literary contexts.

Particles: 探す takes を — the thing you're searching for is the direct object. 鍵を探す (look for the keys). 新しいアパートを探す (look for a new apartment).

Potential が探せる: が — what you can search for becomes the subject. ネットで何でも探せる (you can search for anything online).

Passive に探される: に marks the searcher. 警察に探されている (being searched for by the police).

Causative に…を探させる: に marks who you make search, を marks what. 部下に資料を探させる (have a subordinate look for the documents).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryさがすを
Negativeさがさないを
Politeさがしますを
Polite negさがしませんを
Pastさがしたを
Polite pastさがしましたを
て-formさがしてを
Volitionalさがそうを
Polite volさがしましょうを
Passiveさがされるに
Causativeさがさせるに／を
Caus-passさがさせられるに
Potentialさがせるが
Imperativeさがせを
Imp なさいさがしなさいを
Cond ～ばさがせばを
Cond ～たらさがしたらを

The kanji 紙 has the thread/silk radical 糸 on the left — because the earliest paper in China (invented around 105 AD) was made from plant fibers, silk rags, and old fishing nets. Paper is woven from threads. The right side 氏 was borrowed for its sound, but the fiber connection on the left tells the real story.

かみ sounds identical to 神 (かみ, god) and 髪 (かみ, hair). Three completely different words, same sound. Context always disambiguates: 紙をください (give me paper) is never going to be confused with a request for gods or hair. But this triple homophone is a favorite source of Japanese wordplay.

Japan has a deep paper culture. 和紙 (わし, Japanese paper) — handmade from mulberry bark fibers — is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. It's used for 障子 (shoji screens), calligraphy, origami, and wrapping. The word 折り紙 (おりがみ) is literally "folding paper" — 折る (to fold) + 紙. When a material gets this embedded in the culture, the language builds a whole vocabulary around it.

The kanji 歌 is 可 (doubled, suggesting repeated sounds) plus 欠 — and 欠 is a person with their mouth wide open. A person making repeated sounds with an open mouth: singing. The 欠 radical shows up in other "mouth open" kanji: 飲 (to drink), 吹 (to blow), 歓 (joy, cheering).

歌う is both the verb and, as 歌 (うた), the noun for "song." 歌を歌う (to sing a song) is how you say it — yes, the same kanji appears twice. This repetition sounds natural in Japanese, not redundant. カラオケで歌う (sing at karaoke) is practically a national pastime — the word カラオケ itself is Japanese (空 empty + オーケストラ orchestra).

The older literary form 詠う (also うたう) specifically means to recite poetry — a reminder that singing and poetry were the same art in ancient Japan. 万葉集, Japan's oldest poetry anthology, is literally a collection of 歌. 短歌 (たんか, short poems) and 和歌 (わか, Japanese poems) both use this 歌 — because poems were meant to be sung, not silently read.

Particles: 歌う takes を — the song is the direct object. この歌を歌って (sing this song). You can add で for the venue: カラオケで歌う.

Potential が歌える: が — what you can sing becomes the subject. この曲が歌える？ (can you sing this song?).

Passive に歌われる: に marks the singer. 多くの人に歌われている (sung by many people).

Causative に…を歌わせる: に marks who you make sing, を marks the song. 生徒に国歌を歌わせる (make the students sing the national anthem).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryうたうを
Negativeうたわないを
Politeうたいますを
Polite negうたいませんを
Pastうたったを
Polite pastうたいましたを
て-formうたってを
Volitionalうたおうを
Polite volうたいましょうを
Passiveうたわれるに
Causativeうたわせるに／を
Caus-passうたわせられるに
Potentialうたえるが
Imperativeうたえを
Imp なさいうたいなさいを
Cond ～ばうたえばを
Cond ～たらうたったらを

tags: 速い vs 遅い pair
lesson:
遅い pulls double duty in a way that English splits into two words. "The train is slow" and "you're late" are both 遅い. 電車が遅い (the train is slow / the train is late) — and you genuinely can't tell which meaning without context. The common thread: something isn't arriving at the expected time. Whether that's because it's moving slowly or because it started late, the result is the same.

The kanji 遅 has the movement radical 辶 and a right side that originally depicted a rhinoceros or heavy animal — something that moves ponderously. Movement that drags. The same kanji in 遅刻 (ちこく, being tardy — "late" + "time"), a word that carries real social weight in Japan, where punctuality isn't a suggestion.

遅い vs 遅れる: 遅い is the adjective (something is slow/late). 遅れる (おくれる) is the verb (to become late, to fall behind). 電車が遅い = the train is slow. 電車が遅れている = the train is delayed (something happened). The adjective describes a quality; the verb describes an event.

Dictionaryおそい
Negativeおそくない
Pastおそかった
Past negおそくなかった
Adverbおそく
て-formおそくて

The kanji 首 is one of the most ancient pictographs — in oracle bone script, it was a face with hair on top. Not a neck, but a head. The original meaning was "head," and that meaning still dominates in compounds: 首都 (しゅと, capital city — head city), 首相 (しゅしょう, prime minister — head minister), 首席 (しゅせき, top seat/valedictorian). In modern standalone use, though, くび means the neck — the part that connects the head to the body.

The word has a sharp secondary meaning: getting fired. 首になった (I got sacked — literally "became a neck"). The image is decapitation — your head gets separated from the body of the company. 首にする (to fire someone). It's vivid and common. If your boss says anything about 首, you should pay attention.

首を長くして待つ (to wait with a long neck — to wait eagerly, craning your neck to see if something's coming). 首をかしげる (to tilt one's neck — to be puzzled). 首を突っ込む (to stick your neck in — to poke your nose into other people's business). The neck is surprisingly productive in idioms.

Here's a word that will confuse you until you sort it out: はやい has two kanji — 速い and 早い — and they mean different things. 速い is about speed — how fast something moves. 早い is about time — how early something happens. 車が速い (the car is fast). 朝が早い (the morning is early). In speech they sound identical, and Japanese people themselves sometimes write the wrong one.

The kanji 速 has the movement radical 辶 plus 束 (bundle/bind). The original idea: something bound together and sent along the road — a package being rushed. Movement with urgency. Its opposite is 遅い (slow) — same radical, different payload.

In practice, the distinction between speed-はやい and time-はやい can blur. 返事が速い (the reply is fast — speed) vs 返事が早い (the reply is early — timing). Both are valid and mean slightly different things. When you're unsure, 早い is the safer default — it's used more broadly.

Dictionaryはやい
Negativeはやくない
Pastはやかった
Past negはやくなかった
Adverbはやく
て-formはやくて

一 (one) + 緒 (thread, beginning) = one thread. Things bound by a single thread — together. The 緒 kanji has the silk radical 糸 and originally meant the beginning of a thread, the first strand you pull. 一緒 takes that image and turns it into unity: everything on the same thread.

In daily use, 一緒に is one of the most common phrases you'll hear. 一緒に行こう (let's go together). 一緒に食べない？ (want to eat together?). The に makes it an adverb — it modifies the verb. Without に, 一緒 works as a な-adjective: 同じ学校で一緒だった (we were at the same school together).

一緒 also means "the same" in casual speech. それ、一緒じゃない？ (isn't that the same thing?). This usage overlaps with 同じ, but 一緒 feels more colloquial — you'd use it with friends, not in a paper. The core idea holds: two things are on the same thread, so they're effectively one.

今 (now/this) + 月 (month/moon) = this month. The pattern is mechanical: swap the prefix to get any time reference. 先月 (せんげつ, last month). 来月 (らいげつ, next month). 毎月 (まいつき, every month). Same prefixes work with 週, 年, and others — once you learn the prefix set, you can build any time expression.

Notice the reading: こん + げつ. The 月 becomes げつ (not つき or がつ). This is the on'yomi (Chinese reading) used in compounds. がつ is for specific months (一月, 二月...). つき is the standalone word for moon or month. げつ is for duration and relative time. Three readings, three contexts.

The kanji 今 itself is worth a moment. In oracle bone script, it was a mouth with something being held in — the idea of "right now, at this moment," something being contained in the present. It shows up constantly: 今日 (きょう, today), 今年 (ことし, this year), 今度 (こんど, next time / this time).

遊ぶ is broader than English "play." Kids playing at the park? 遊ぶ. Hanging out with friends on a Saturday? 遊ぶ. Goofing off instead of working? 遊ぶ. Going on a trip for fun? 遊ぶ. The word covers any leisure activity that isn't work or duty. 明日遊ばない？ doesn't mean "want to play?" in a childish way — it means "want to hang out?" Adults use it constantly.

The kanji 遊 has the movement radical 辶 and a right side that originally depicted a child carrying a flag or banner — moving freely, wandering without purpose. That aimless wandering is the heart of 遊ぶ: you're not going somewhere specific, you're just... out, free, enjoying yourself.

Cultural note: 遊びに行く (to go out to have fun / to go visit someone) is the standard invitation and the standard way to describe going to a friend's place. うちに遊びに来てね (come hang out at my place). The に after 遊び marks purpose — you're going in order to play/hang out.

Particles: 遊ぶ is intransitive — you don't play something, you just play. No を needed for the basic meaning. Use で for where you play: 公園で遊ぶ (play at the park). Use と for who you play with: 友達と遊ぶ (hang out with friends).

Passive に遊ばれる: に marks who toyed with you. あの人に遊ばれた (I was played by that person — romantically led on). A pointed usage.

Causative を／に遊ばせる: を when you let or make someone play: 子供を外で遊ばせる (let the kids play outside). に leans more permissive.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryあそぶで／と
Negativeあそばないで／と
Politeあそびますで／と
Polite negあそびませんで／と
Pastあそんだで／と
Polite pastあそびましたで／と
て-formあそんでで／と
Volitionalあそぼうで／と
Polite volあそびましょうで／と
Passiveあそばれるに
Causativeあそばせるを／に
Caus-passあそばせられるに
Potentialあそべるで／と
Imperativeあそべで／と
Imp なさいあそびなさいで／と
Cond ～ばあそべばで／と
Cond ～たらあそんだらで／と

The kanji 遠 has the movement radical 辶 wrapping around a complex right side that includes 口 (mouth) and elements suggesting a garment or enclosure. The idea: movement that keeps going, stretching away from you, beyond the boundary. Distance as a road that won't end.

とおい works for physical and metaphorical distance alike. 駅は遠い (the station is far). 遠い昔 (the distant past — long ago). 遠い親戚 (a distant relative). 目が遠い (far-sighted — can't see things close up). Notice how the opposite 近い (ちかい, near) maps to the same domains: 近い親戚 (close relative), 近い将来 (near future).

Pay attention to the pronunciation: とおい, not とうい. The middle sound is a true お, not an う. This is one of the few common words where おお (double お) isn't written as おう — a spelling irregularity that trips up learners. 大きい (おおきい) does the same thing. Just memorize these as exceptions.

Dictionaryとおい
Negativeとおくない
Pastとおかった
Past negとおくなかった
Adverbとおく
て-formとおくて
